summary: Gruber argues that Mozilla's refusal to support H.264 video in Firefox, in favor of the open but technically inferior Ogg Theora, is a strategically self-defeating position. With IE9, Safari, and Chrome all supporting H.264, Firefox's holdout will simply result in its users being served Flash video instead of HTML5 — ironically using a closed plugin to play H.264-encoded video anyway. He emphasizes that mobile computing, where dedicated H.264 hardware decoding is essential for battery life, makes Ogg Theora completely impractical. Gruber concludes that Mozilla's irrelevance in mobile computing blinds them to the forces driving H.264 adoption, and that being 'not a mobile company' is increasingly synonymous with being irrelevant.
key_points: Three of four major browsers (IE9, Safari, Chrome) support H.264, leaving Firefox as the sole holdout among major browsers
Mozilla's stance will not drive Ogg Theora adoption — publishers will simply serve Flash to Firefox users while giving HTML5 video to everyone else
Mobile devices rely on dedicated H.264 hardware decoding for practical battery life, making Ogg Theora completely unacceptable for mobile
Mozilla's lack of mobile presence means they don't feel the pressure that's driving H.264 adoption industry-wide
'Open but worse' is a recipe for obscurity — people embrace free software only when it matches or exceeds proprietary alternatives
money_quotes: Put another way, "open and better" is a recipe for success; "open but worse" is a recipe for obscurity.

Mozilla's intransigence in the name of "openness" will result in Firefox users being served video using the closed Flash Player plugin, and behind the scenes the video is likely to be encoded using H.264 anyway.
Even those using the latest version of Firefox will be treated like they're using a legacy browser.
At this point, "not a mobile company" is getting hard to distinguish from "not a relevant company".
Ogg Theora may well be "good enough" for desktop computers, but it's completely unacceptable for mobile devices.
I think this explains why Microsoft is throwing its support behind H.264 rather than some proprietary video codec of its own — Microsoft knows a winning position when it sees one.
All of you using a Nokia Maemo, please raise your hands. Crickets.
key_insight: Mozilla's idealistic stand for Ogg Theora over H.264 is rendered futile by mobile computing's hardware realities, and their absence from mobile means they can't even see the forces that are making their position irrelevant.
